A fun experiment in DOM proximity detection, Euclidean distance math, and responsive web controls without external frameworks.
Every web developer loves a classic “useless web” toy. From HackerTyper to PointerPointer, single-purpose interactive pages are great exercises in pure, framework-free web development.
To continue my series on lightweight single-page tools and web experiments, I built The Patience Test — a simple web app where a big red button actively dodges your mouse cursor or finger taps before you can click it.
Here is a breakdown of how the proximity math works under the hood and how to handle responsiveness across desktop and mobile devices.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Brands&People on Unsplash1. Calculating Proximity Using Euclidean Distance
Instead of waiting for the user’s cursor to physically hover over or click the button (which is often too slow), the script calculates the distance between the mouse coordinates $(x_1, y_1)$ and the center of the button $(x_2, y_2)$ on every mousemove event.
Using the Pythagorean theorem:
$$\text{distance} = \sqrt{(x_2 — x_1)² + (y_2 — y_1)²}$$
